They didn’t believe that people like my parents should be able to come to America to build a better future for their families, and routinely put obstacles in the way of immigrants achieving the American dream.

Helping ensure immigrants can come to America and be treated with dignity and respect has been a key part of my life’s work, and as the son of immigrants, watching the former president attack people like my parents shook me to my core.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Restrooms should be off limits: Why political protests from all sides are out of control

That’s why it was so easy for me to throw my support behind Joe Biden in the 2020 election – I have seen his hard work on immigration reform as a senator and knew he had a deep respect for the Latino community. And since he was sworn in as president, Joe Biden has taken crucial action to reform our immigration system and help Latino families get back on their feet.

From Day One, President Biden has worked to rebuild our economy and help those who have struggled as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic, and he has put bold plans in place to help solve the systemic problems we as a nation have faced for decades.

During Hispanic Heritage Month, I am especially grateful for all of the work President Biden has done on behalf of the Latino community. Thanks to his swift action, millions of Latino Americans have the support they need to get back on their feet after the pandemic ravaged their homes and businesses, including thousands of dollars in their pockets from the president’s expanded child tax credit that kicked in this July.

While this has been a strong start to Biden’s presidency, there is much more work to do to ensure a better future for Latino families. And that means both ending the filibuster and passing swift, meaningful immigration reform that will put more people on a path to citizenship.

Fighting for immigration reform

Immigration reform will be good for our families, our communities and our economy as a whole. But for too long, bold plans like this have been stalled, cast aside or marred by red tape. In 2013, the Democratic-controlled Senate by a 68-32 vote passed comprehensive immigration reform that would have provided a pathway to citizenship for over 11 million immigrants, as well as reforms to ensure that we would be a nation of laws and a nation of immigrants.

Then-Vice President Biden played an integral role in the passage of this bill in the Senate, just as he did in 1996 when the last immigration reform bill was enacted.

The Republican-controlled House never allowed this bill to come up for a vote. The party of Lincoln and Reagan was once the party that embraced our immigrant heritage. The party of Trump is the party of nativism.

Christian Arana: Why California Latinos hold the key to the 2022 midterm elections

There is still a long way to go. The crisis we have seen at the border over the past several months has proved that it will take everyone, from both sides of the aisle, coming together to enact real reform. President Biden and Democrats have proved that they are on the right side of this fight. It’s time for Republicans to get on board.

On his first day in office, President Biden sent a bill to Congress that would modernize our immigration system and create a path to citizenship for those who come to our country for opportunity. It’s time for our leaders to do right by the millions of Latino Americans who call our country home and get this bill passed.

I’ll never forget when my father said to me and my siblings, “You’re so lucky to be in America. Leave the ladder down for those who come after you.”

Since January and especially now in Hispanic Heritage month, I have been excited to have voted for a president who shares my values when it comes to supporting the Latino community. Joe Biden has put more money in the pockets of Latino families who were struggling to get back on their feet after the pandemic hit, and has taken crucial steps toward strengthening our immigration system. The best way for our leaders in Congress to celebrate Hispanic Heritage Month is to finish the job.

Tom Perez serves as co-chair of American Bridge 21st Century. He is a former chairman of the Democratic National Committee.

search_result.title: 'Big Beautiful Bill': Are there no taxes on overtime, tips?
search_result.text:
Updated May 23, 2025, 12:45 p.m. ET

The "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" narrowly passed the House and is now under Senate review.

The bill proposes eliminating taxes on overtime pay and tips, but analysis suggests it primarily benefits high-income earners.

President Donald Trump "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" was narrowly passed by the U.S. House on May 22. Here's what it means for Americans.

Though the bill was known to many for its aim to end federal taxes on tips and overtime, its 1,116 pages have clearly defined limits as to who will benefit most from its contents. According to analysis from several sources, the group that will see the most benefits will be the country's top earners.

After intense Republican negotiations, the U.S. House passed the bill by a vote of 215-214. It is now headed for the Senate, where revisions are planned.

Here's what to know about Trump's "Big Beautiful Bill" as it heads for the Senate floor.

Does the 'Big Beautiful Bill' have no tax on overtime? No tax on tips?

Yes, overtime and tips were covered in the bill.

Overtime wages - Currently treated like regular wages, overtime wages are subject to federal and state income taxes, Social Security and Medicare withholding. If the bill passes, they would no longer be taxed. The Tax Foundation and Yale's Budget Lab found in April that federal revenue could be reduced by $680-$866 billion from 2025 to 2034 if overtime pay wasn't taxed.

Tips - Additionally, tips would not be taxed if the bill passes. This provision would end after 2028.

Is no tax on Social Security part of the 2025 bill?

No, Social Security programs cannot be changed through this budget reconciliation process, according to The Hill.

However, the bill includes tax relief for seniors - an extra $4,000 deduction for people over 65 filing their taxes, according to CBS News - which Republicans have promoted alongside other campaign promises like no tax on tips and no tax on overtime.

Nonpartisan nonprofit the Tax Foundation categorizes the standard deduction for seniors as a bad thing, saying it "(violates) basic tax principles of treating taxpayers equally."

Who benefits from the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act?'

Analysis from USA TODAY compiled a list of groups who will benefit the most from the act. The list included:

High-income earners: The bill “would cut taxes on average by about $2,800 in 2026,” according to an analysis by the nonpartisan Tax Policy Center. More than two-thirds of the total cuts would go to those with annual incomes of about $217,000 or more, the center said. Those with incomes of $1.1 million or more would get nearly a fourth of the cuts.

The bill “would cut taxes on average by about $2,800 in 2026,” according to an analysis by the nonpartisan Tax Policy Center. More than two-thirds of the total cuts would go to those with annual incomes of about $217,000 or more, the center said. Those with incomes of $1.1 million or more would get nearly a fourth of the cuts. Families with children: The bill would increase the child tax credit by $500 to $2,500 through 2028. It would drop to $2,000 after that. But an estimated 4.5 million children become ineligible under a new requirement that both parents have a Social Security number, USA TODAY reported. Children younger than 8 would be given $1,000 each for their parents to open "money accounts for growth and investment," also known as a "MAGA" savings account. That has been renamed to Trump Accounts.

But an estimated 4.5 million children become ineligible under a new requirement that both parents have a Social Security number, USA TODAY reported. Children younger than 8 would be given $1,000 each for their parents to open "money accounts for growth and investment," also known as a "MAGA" savings account. That has been renamed to Trump Accounts. Car buyers: The bill also would allow people to temporarily deduct up to $10,000 in car loan interest payments if they buy an American-made vehicle.

The bill also would allow people to temporarily deduct up to $10,000 in car loan interest payments if they buy an American-made vehicle. Those with overtime pay: Overtime wages, which are treated like regular wages with federal and state income taxes, Social Security and Medicare withholding, would not be taxed if the bill passes. Federal revenue could be reduced by $680 billion to $866 billion from 2025 to 2034 if overtime pay wasn't taxed, according to a study by the Tax Foundation and Yale’s Budget Lab in April.

Overtime wages, which are treated like regular wages with federal and state income taxes, Social Security and Medicare withholding, would not be taxed if the bill passes. Federal revenue could be reduced by $680 billion to $866 billion from 2025 to 2034 if overtime pay wasn't taxed, according to a study by the Tax Foundation and Yale’s Budget Lab in April. Waiters and workers who get tips: Tips would not be taxed if the bill passes. Tips are historically underreported, according to the IRS. Unreported tip income from noncompliant businesses could be as high as $23 billion, according to a report from the Treasury Inspector General for Tax Administration in 2018. The "no tax on tips" provision would end after 2028.

Who does the 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' hurt?

On the other end of the spectrum, USA TODAY found that there were also plenty of groups who would be negatively affected by the act. The top five groups included:

Those making less than $50,000: Americans making about $17,000 to $51,000 would lose about $700. Those with an income of less than $17,000 would lose more than $1,000 on average. The losses are mainly a result of cuts in assistance programs including Medicaid, health insurance marketplaces, the Supplemental Nutritional Assistance Program and student loans.

Americans making about $17,000 to $51,000 would lose about $700. Those with an income of less than $17,000 would lose more than $1,000 on average. The losses are mainly a result of cuts in assistance programs including Medicaid, health insurance marketplaces, the Supplemental Nutritional Assistance Program and student loans. SNAP/Medicaid recipients: The bill's changes to Medicaid could result in as many as 7.6 million Americans losing health insurance over the next 10 years, according to initial estimates by the CBO. About $698 billion would be cut from the program. The measure would cut $267 billion in federal spending for Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, known as SNAP or food stamps, CBO said. It would also impose work requirements on those age 55 to 64 who benefit from the program, which provides food assistance to about 42 million Americans.

About $698 billion would be cut from the program. The measure would cut $267 billion in federal spending for Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, known as SNAP or food stamps, CBO said. It would also impose work requirements on those age 55 to 64 who benefit from the program, which provides food assistance to about 42 million Americans. People with student loan debt: Student loan relief regulations enacted by President Joe Biden's administration would be repealed, and the number of repayment plans for federally held loans would shrink to just two programs. The bill would also impose significant caps on loans for parents and undergraduate students, while eliminating a lending program for future graduate students.

The bill would also impose significant caps on loans for parents and undergraduate students, while eliminating a lending program for future graduate students. Higher federal deficit: The bill's provisions would increase the federal deficit by $3.8 trillion from 2026 to 2034, according to the CBO, which cited tax changes, including the extension of the 2017 tax act and its revenue and outlays for refundable credits.

The bill's provisions would increase the federal deficit by $3.8 trillion from 2026 to 2034, according to the CBO, which cited tax changes, including the extension of the 2017 tax act and its revenue and outlays for refundable credits. Undocumented people: The bill would increase fees for legal immigration. It would impose a $1,000 fee on requests for asylum and require $500 payments for work authorizations every six months, USA TODAY reported. It would charge immigrants hundreds of dollars if they appeal court decisions, among other fees. The bill would also discourage states from using their own money to provide Medicaid coverage to undocumented children.

North Carolina's 'No Tax on Tips, Overtime, Bonus Pay' Bill

In North Carolina, state lawmakers are working toward their own version of a bill addressing taxes on tips, overtime and bonus wages. House Bill 11, which was recently advanced by the House Commerce Committee, aims to exempt all income taxes on tips, overtime pay and the first $2,500 of annual bonuses.

Though these proposals may sound like easy money to some, HB 11 has its fair share of problems, according to the Tax Foundation and some similar organizations.

"While pitched as relief for low-wage service workers and hourly employees, this approach is economically distortive, poorly targeted, and risks unintended fiscal consequences," wrote Senior Policy Analyst Abir Mandal in a recent article on the bill.

The same article stated that HB 11 has "limited reach and arbitrary benefits," explaining that a large majority of low-income workers are not tipped workers, and not all tipped workers are low-income. In fact, data shows that only about 125,000 of NC's 4.9 million workers are tipped.

While about 55% of workers could qualify for overtime exemption, the Tax Foundation says that favoring specific income types - in this case tips, overtime, bonuses - "lacks a coherent policy rationale and is distributionally erratic, as many professions with overtime pay are well-compensated."

The article also warned of economic distortions and compliance costs. "Overtime could become cheaper than new hires, as employers avoid fixed costs like benefits, or more attractive due to its tax-free status." In this case, job creation could be crowded out and employment agreements could become unnecessarily complicated.

"Worse, without safeguards, high-earners might reclassify income as “voluntary tips” to exploit the exemption, further eroding the tax base. Even with guardrails, compliance costs would rise, undermining North Carolina’s simple flat-tax system," Mandal wrote.

CONTRIBUTING: Riley Beggins, Bailey Schulz, Lauren Villagran Zachary Schermele, Chris Quintana and Dan Morrison

SOURCE: USA TODAY Network reporting and research; Reuters; Tax Policy Center; Penn Wharton at the University of Pennsylvania; Center on Budget and Policy Priorities; Congressional Budget Office

Iris Seaton is the trending news reporter for the Asheville Citizen Times, part of the USA TODAY Network. Reach her at iseaton@citizentimes.com.

search_result.title: Opinion: Businesses strike back as DeSantis criticizes corporate ...
search_result.text:
Editor’s Note: Ruth Ben-Ghiat (@ruthbenghiat), a frequent contributor to CNN Opinion, is professor of history and Italian studies at New York University and the author of “Strongmen: Mussolini to the Present.” She publishes the newsletter Lucid on threats to democracy. The views expressed here are her own. Read more opinion on CNN.

CNN —

“Florida is fighting back,” tweeted Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis on Aug. 22. “We stand for the values of places like Destin, Dunedin and Deland - not Davos.”

Who is the enemy DeSantis is combatting? “Woke CEOs” and any “corporate power” that aims to impose “an ideological agenda on the American people” by championing values of “diversity, inclusion, and equity” in investment considerations and workplace policies.

Ruth Ben-Ghiat CNN

Going after corporate America might seem a surprising move for a Yale-educated lawyer who will likely run for president in 2024 and is backed by more than 40 billionaire donors. But having traction as a Republican candidate in many cases means posing as a defender of freedoms threatened by political correctness and the machinations of “corporate cartel elites.” DeSantis apparently is fine with harming Florida’s reputation as a business-friendly state, if that’s what it takes to make his mark and impose his political will.

Yet businesses are pushing back. DeSantis has faced a “summer of litigation” that has complicated his efforts to mandate what businesses can and cannot say and do with their own employees. Florida is now a test case for the response of American business to government interference in the private sector.

His contrarian crusade in April to strip the job-creating giant Disney of its special self-governance status, regardless of the potential costs to Florida taxpayers, is one example. (DeSantis has insisted that taxpayers won’t shoulder any of the Disney World district’s debt.) The company became a target for speaking out against what critics call his “Don’t Say Gay” bill, which bans K-3 classroom discussions of gender identity and sexual orientation and allows parents to potentially sue school districts that engage these topics. The governor’s actions generated multiple recent lawsuits, which DeSantis and his state agencies have filed motions to dismiss.

And earlier this month, a Florida judge blocked parts of the Stop WOKE Act, which restricts discussions of race and racial discrimination in the education and business sectors.

DeSantis is by no means alone in this quest to discipline the private sector. Punishing corporations and organizations that take stands against Republican illiberal measures is now part of the GOP playbook, as Delta Airlines discovered, when it nearly lost a jet fuel tax break worth $35 million for expressing opposition to a new and highly restrictive Georgia voting law.

Yet DeSantis has arguably been the most activist governor, attempting to bully sports teams, state retirement fund managers that use environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in choosing assets, and even the Special Olympics if their actions express a commitment to values and platforms he disagrees with – such as gun control, vaccine mandates or climate change.

That’s why businesses are striking back. A lawsuit brought by wedding registry Honeyfund.com and the diversity consultancies Collective Concepts and Whitespace Consulting argued that Florida’s “Stop the Wrongs to Our Kids and Employees Act” (the Stop WOKE Act), which challenges the implementation of diversity training programs and exposes corporations that offer them to legal risks, is unconstitutional in its curtailing of free speech.

A Florida judge agreed, which is why he blocked the Stop WOKE Act. U.S. District Judge Mark Walker struck the law down and refused to issue a stay to keep it in effect during any appeals by the state.

“If Florida truly believes we live in a post-racial society then let it make its case,” Walker wrote. “But it cannot win the argument by muzzling its opponents.”

Free speech concerns also led to the defeat of much of a DeSantis measure to fine technology companies if they deplatform political candidates. While the governor leaned hard into the populist rhetoric, calling Twitter and other corporations “elites” and “Big Tech censors” pushing “the dominant Silicon Valley ideology.” Circuit Judge Kevin Newsom, a Trump appointee, was having none of it.

“Put simply, with minor exceptions, the government can’t tell a private person or entity what to say or how to say it,” Newsom ruled, finding that social media companies are “private actors” protected by the First Amendment.

DeSantis’ actions against ESG investing suggest that Florida businesses are perhaps pawns in his ambitious plans to run for president. On Aug. 23, he passed a resolution that eliminates ESG considerations from Florida state pension fund investments.

“[It] will direct Florida’s fund managers to make sound investments on returns not woke ideology. Environmental, Social & Governance (ESG) is dead on arrival in Florida,” the governor announced.

This strongman rhetoric hides the fact that the whole declaration was a performance. As Kent Perez, spokesman for the State Board of Administration that directs the pension fund remarked, Florida’s fund was not currently investing with ESG factors in mind. “We do not invest to make social statements,” Perez said.

Others appointed by Republicans are equally baffled.

Follow CNN Opinion Join us on Twitter and Facebook

“Sadly, [the law] moves us one step closer to authoritarian government,” says Marcos Daniel Jiménez, who served as a US Attorney for the Southern District of Florida during the George W. Bush administration. “Targeting the speech of private employers based on the beliefs and preferences of current lawmakers” places “American liberty” and “our system of free enterprise” in jeopardy, Jiménez concludes.

But that’s okay with DeSantis, whose message that Florida is a “free state” with respect to the federal government’s “authoritarian, arbitrary, and seemingly never-ending mandates and restrictions” covers up his own desire to police speech and behavior.

As DeSantis prepares to run for president as a strongman-style leader, he will undoubtedly escalate his attempts to domesticate the private sector. It’ll be up to businesses in Florida to lead the way in protecting their rights against autocratic overreach.

search_result.title: Florida golfer Tiger Woods in rollover crash on Jupiter Island, arrested
search_result.text:
Updated March 27, 2026, 6:13 p.m. ET

Tiger Woods was involved in a rollover crash on Jupiter Island on the afternoon of March 27, according to Martin County sheriff's officials.

The crash occurred just after 2 p.m. in the 200 block of Beach Road, officials said. The extent of any injuries to the 50-year-old professional golfer or if anyone else was involved was unknown.

News conference highlights from Sheriff John Budensiek:

∎ Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek says a pressure cleaner truck was northbound, turning into a driveway, and the driver saw a Land Rover overtaking him at high speeds. He edged off side of Beach Road. Land Rover swerved into back end of trailer. It was a 30 mph zone.

∎ Land Rover driver identified as Tiger Woods, 50. He did not seem to be injured. He was arrested and charged with DUI and property damage and taken to Martin County Jail. He will be there for at least 8 hours then released on bond.

∎ Woods crawled out of the passenger window. He showed signs of impairment.

∎ DUI experts evaluated him on scene; they were not concerned with alcohol.

∎ He cooperated, but careful of what he said or didn't say. Not sure if wearing a seat belt. No on else in his car.

∎ Woods refused urine test. Cooperated with breathalizer test.

∎ We will never get definitive answer on what he was impaired with, Budensiek said, because he refused urine test.

∎ No drugs or medication found.

∎ No injuries to driver of pressure washer truck.

Tiger Woods' home is in Jupiter Island, where he's lived since 2007, when he purchased the property for $44.5 million, according to Martin County property appraiser records.

At the Martin County Jail, the intake clerk said Woods had not be booked yet as of 5 p.m.

The golf legend was previously involved in a serious rollover crash in California in 2021. He was arrested in 2017 on charges of driving under the influence in Jupiter.

After another single-car crash in Florida in 2009, Woods was found unconscious and snoring at the scene, according to a USA TODAY article. Woods later acknowledged he used Ambien but didn’t directly answer when asked at a news conference if Ambien played a role in that crash.

Laurie K. Blandford is a breaking news reporter with TCPalm. Email her at laurie.blandford@tcpalm.com.

search_result.title: Tiger Woods released from jail after DUI arrest following car crash
search_result.text:
March 27, 2026 Updated March 28, 2026, 4:36 p.m. ET

Tiger Woods was arrested for driving under the influence after a car crash in Florida on Friday, March 27, the Martin County Sheriff's Office said. Woods and the other driver involved in the two-car crash were not injured.

Woods was released from the Martin County Jail late Friday night.

The crash took place in Jupiter Island, Florida. Mark Steinberg, Woods' longtime agent, did not immediately respond to a request for information about Woods' condition.

The golf legend was previously involved in a serious rollover crash in California in 2021.

In addition to his DUI charge, Woods is also facing charges for property damage and refusal to submit to lawful test. Woods was not under the influence of alcohol – his breathalyzer test registered a 0.00, police said – but deputies determined Woods was impaired. He did not submit to a urine test.

Woods, 50, recently indicated his playing at The Masters was a possibility as he continued to recover from an Achilles injury and back surgery.

On March 24, Woods played in the TGL Finals at the SoFi Center in Palm Beach Gardens but took nine shots.

Golfer Tiger Woods was released from the Martin County Jail in Stuart, Florida, late March 27.

Woods was arrested on suspicion of a DUI after crawling out of his rolled-over vehicle following a two-vehicle crash on Jupiter Island after 2 p.m. ET on March 27, according to Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek.

Woods was reportedly released at 11:15 p.m. ET. The 50-year-old man was seen leaving the side entrance of the jail, riding in the passenger seat of an SUV.

Woods was expected to be detained for at least eight hours following his arrest and had to post bail as part of protocol in the state, according to Budensiek.

Budensiek stated during a March 27 press conference that Woods was cooperative following the crash. — James H. Williams

Has Tiger Woods been released from jail?

As of 11 p.m. ET, the Martin County Sheriff's Office would not confirm whether Woods was still being held in jail or if the golfer was already released on bail when USA TODAY Sports contacted the office by telephone.

Woods was not listed in the jail’s online inmate search, which states it was last updated at 6:16 p.m. ET.

Woods was arrested on suspicion of a DUI on Friday after crawling out of his rolled-over vehicle on Jupiter Island after 2 p.m. ET, according to Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek.

Woods was expected to be detained for at least eight hours, meaning the earliest the golfer could be released from jail – after posting bail – is 11 p.m. ET, since he was booked into jail around 3 p.m., according to authorities.

Budensiek stated during the press conference that Woods was cooperative following the accident. — James H. Williams

Did authorities find drugs in Tiger Woods' vehicle?

Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek stated that Woods was impaired, but drugs or medication were not found at the scene.

What criminal allegations is Tiger Woods facing?

Tiger Woods emerged unhurt after a rollover car crash in Jupiter Island, Florida, on May 27, and is expected to serve at least eight hours in the Martin County jail.

Woods "crawled out of the passenger window" after the car rolled over, according to Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek. It is not known if he was wearing a seatbelt.

If he is convicted of any wrongdoing, he could face fines and more jail time.

The penalties for a first conviction of DUI are imprisonment for up to six months and a fine of up to $1,000.

A first refusal to submit to a lawful test is punishable by up to 60 days in jail and a $500 fine, according to Florida statute.

Lynch: Tiger Woods deserves empathy, but needs to show responsibility

The life of Tiger Woods now seems destined to be cemented into two narrative chapters. Golfweek's Eamon Lynch on the highs and lows in the legendary golfer's life.

Armour: Tiger Woods needs help. His latest crash must be his last

Columnist Nancy Armour writes, "He needs to get clean and he needs tools to help him stay that way. Woods is fortunate he's only facing DUI allegations and not something worse. If there's a next time, he might not be so lucky."

President Trump sends well wishes to Tiger Woods

President Donald Trump sent well wishes to Tiger Woods, following the golfer's car accident on Friday afternoon.

"He's got some difficulty. There was an accident, and that's all I know," Trump said of Woods' incident. "Very close friend of mine. He's an amazing person. Amazing man. But some difficulty. I don't want to talk about it."

Trump said he and Woods were close friends. The 50-year-old golfer is dating Vanessa Trump, 48, the ex-wife of Trump's son, Donald Trump Jr.

How much is Tiger Woods worth?

Tiger Woods is worth $1.5 billion, according to Forbes. Woods has earned roughly $1.8 billion, before taxes, during his pro golf career. He set a PGA Tour record, winning $121 million in prize money.

Tiger Woods DUI could lead to suspended license

Tiger Woods could have his driver's license suspended because he refused to take a urinalysis following his rollover crash, according to The Palm Beach Post, part of the USA TODAY Network.

Florida House Bill 687, known as "Trenton's Law,' criminalizes the first refusal of a urine test during a DUI arrest, making it a second-degree misdemeanor punishable by up to 60 days in jail and license suspension.

The law became effective Oct. 1, 2025. — Tom D'Angelo, Palm Beach Post

Why was Tiger Woods charged with DUI?

The Drug Recognition Expert (DRE) on the scene believed Woods’ impairment stemmed from “some kind of medication or drug,’’ according to Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek.

While a breath test showed that Woods was not under the influence of alcohol, he refused a urinalysis test and was charged with a misdemeanor.

Tiger Woods car crash history

His use of the sleep drug Ambien came into question in three previous incidents with his vehicles. After rolling his SUV in Los Angeles County in February 2021, authorities didn’t have his blood drawn to test for drugs. But experts said the evidence supported the theory that Woods was not conscious when he drove off the road, didn't brake and hit a tree before rolling over. Woods suffered broken bones in his leg but wasn't even issued a traffic citation for that incident.

In May 2017, Woods was charged with a DUI in Jupiter, Florida, after police found his Mercedes stopped on the road in the right lane with its brake lights on and the right blinker still blinking. The police report said, “Woods had extremely slow and slurred speech." A toxicology report showed he had five drugs in his system then: Vicodin, Dilaudid, Xanax, Ambien and THC.

In November 2009, Woods was behind the wheel when his Cadillac Escalade collided with a row of hedges and hit a fire hydrant and a tree before stopping outside his mansion in Windermere, Florida.

A neighbor at the scene of the 2009 incident outside of his home reported seeing Woods unconscious and snoring, according to a police report.

But the Florida Highway Patrol determined then that there “was insufficient evidence available to issue a subpoena for additional medical information that may exist in this case.”

Woods was cited for careless driving and fined $164. A sex scandal involving Woods ensued after that. — Brent Schrotenboer

Was Tiger Woods injured?

Woods and the other driver involved in the two-car crash were not injured, authorities said during a news conference on Friday, March 27. Woods did not have a passenger in his vehicle.

Follow any further developments on Wood's injury status here:

What happened in Tiger Woods car crash?

Woods was driving a Land Rover northbound, as was a truck pulling a trailer with a pressure cleaner. Police said video surveillance showed Woods’ Land Rover overtaking the truck at a high speed before Woods clipped the back of the truck.

The speed limit was 30 mph and Woods’ car never fully rolled over, said Martin County Sheriff John Budensiek.

“This could have been a lot worse,” he said.

Budensiek also said Woods slid "for a pretty decent space" before stopping.

Where did Tiger Woods crash happen?

According to local police, the March 27 crash occurred just after 2 p.m. in the 200 block of Beach Road in Jupiter Island, Florida. The crash occurred about three miles from Woods' home, according to public property records. Tiger Woods' home is in Jupiter Island, where he's lived since 2007, when he purchased the property for $44.5 million, according to Martin County property appraiser records.

Tiger Woods crash news conference

Watch the video of the Martin County officials’ news conference regarding the accident involving Woods.

Donald Trump says Tiger Woods won’t play at 2026 Masters

During an appearance Thursday on Fox News, president Donald Trump said Woods would not be playing at the 2026 Masters. Woods had previously said he was doing everything he can to be in shape for the four days at Augusta National.

Woods underwent lumbar disc replacement surgery in his L4/5 Lumbar spine for lower back symptoms back in October. He hasn't competed on the PGA Tour since July 2024.

Golf star Tiger Woods was arressted on DUI charges after being involved in a rollover car crash in Florida on Friday, March 27.

This event follows a severe 2021 crash in Los Angeles that seriously injured his leg.

Golf great Tiger Woods, 50, was arrested after he was involved in a rollover car crash in Florida Friday, March 27.

According to the Martin County Sheriff's Office, the crash happened shortly after 2 p.m., near Woods' home on Beach Road on Jupiter Island.

According to the Martin County Sheriff's Office, the driver of a truck pulling a pressure washer said he saw a Land Rover approaching from behind at a high rate of speed. The truck driver attempted to move toward the side of the road but his trailer was clipped by the Land Rover, driven by Woods.

Woods was reportedly able to climb out of the passenger window of the Land Rover. He was uninjured, according to authorities.

According to the sheriff's office, investigators at the scene suspected Woods was impaired, not by alcohol, but by medication or drugs. He was arrested and taken to the Martin County Sheriff's Office, where he was administered an alcohol breath test, which was negative. Woods refused a urinalysis. He was charged with DUI and booked into the Martin County Jail.

Woods, who has been recovering from a 2025 Achilles injury, recently made his golf season debut at the TGL Finals in Los Angeles.

Here's what we know about Tiger Woods' rollover crash in Florida:

Was Tiger Woods in a car crash?

Yes. According to the Martin County Sheriff's Office, Woods was involved in a rollover crash near his home on Jupiter Island.

Was Tiger Woods injured in rollover crash?

According to the Martin County Sheriff's Office, Woods was not injured in the crash.

Where is Jupiter Island, Florida?

The crash happened in the 200 block of Beach Road, on Jupiter Island, Florida. Woods' home is in the 400 block of Beach Road.

Jupiter Island is a small barrier island north of Miami and Palm Beach, home to celebrities, athletes and investors.

Tiger Woods severely injured legs in 2021 Los Angeles crash

Woods sustained serious leg injuries in a February 2021 one-car crash in the Los Angeles area.

Woods had to be extricated from his vehicle after the crash, in which his Genesis GV80 struck the median of a street in Rancho Palos Verdes, California, rolled over multiple times and landed in the brush several hundred feet away. Woods, then 45, was the only person in the car.

Trump announced Tiger Woods will not play in 2026 Masters at Augusta

“I love Tiger, but he won’t be there," Trump said. "Well, he’ll be there, but he won’t be playing in it."

Tiger Woods, and Vanessa Trump, the former wife of Donald Trump Jr., are dating and made their relationship "Instagram official" on March 23, 2025.

search_result.title: Tiger Woods injury history after DUI rollover crash in Florida
search_result.text:
Updated March 27, 2026, 6:59 p.m. ET

Tiger Woods was involved in a rollover car crash in Florida but was not injured.

The golfer was charged with DUI and property damage following the two-vehicle collision.

Woods has a long history of injuries and surgeries, primarily affecting his knees, back, and Achilles tendon.

Previous incidents include a 2021 car crash that resulted in severe leg and ankle injuries.

Golfing legend Tiger Woods was involved in a rollover vehicle crash March 27 near his home in Jupiter Island, Florida. According to the local sheriff, Woods – nor anyone else – was injured in the two-vehicle collision. Woods was charged with DUI and property damage as a result of the incident.

While Woods sits in jail facing his latest round of legal issues, his body apparently at least avoided another round of damage after the extensive array it has already endured.

Woods’ incandescent career – his 15 major titles are second only to Jack Nicklaus (18) – has been repeatedly interrupted by injuries suffered on the golf course and away from it. Here’s a look back at them:

1994 knee surgery

Scar tissue was removed from Woods’ left knee while he was still playing collegiately at Stanford.

2001 knee procedure

Woods had fluid drained from the left knee following the PGA season.

2007 ACL tear

Woods tore the ligament while running in the summer of 2007. He continued to play through it that year.

2008 knee surgery

Woods had arthroscopic surgery after the Masters in 2008. He required another operation following the U.S. Open that year, partially to repair his ACL, and missed the rest of that season.

2009 Achilles injury

Woods revealed he had to manage multiple Achilles issues during the 2009 season as he was coming back from his knee surgery.

2011 Achilles, knee woes

Woods suffered more Achilles and knee problems while playing in the 2011 Masters and withdrew from multiple events afterward.

2014: Back issues begin

After limiting his schedule early in the year with back problems, Woods missed the Masters after undergoing a microdiscectomy for a pinched nerve.

2015: More back surgery

Woods had another microdiscectomy procedure in September of 2015. He wound up missing the entire 2016 PGA season.

2017 spinal fusion surgery

A major back operation in the spring of 2017 was intended to relieve pain in Woods’ back and leg. He only played in one tournament across the 2016 and ’17 seasons.

2019: More knee surgery

Woods has a fifth operation on his left knee in August 2019.

2021: Fifth back operation

Woods underwent a fourth microdiscectomy in January – the fifth procedure to his back overall at that point – in the latest bid to alleviate his chronic pain.

2021 car crash

While recovering from latest procedure to his back, Woods suffered a compound fracture to his right leg and a severely injured ankle in a single-car crash in Los Angeles on February 23, 2021. The damage to his body effectively ended his career as a competitive golfer.

Woods' history with Ambien: Tiger Woods has history with car crashes, DUI incidents before latest in Florida

2023 ankle surgery

Shortly after withdrawing from the Masters in a failed bid to return to major competition, Woods requires further surgery on the ankle that was severely damaged from the 2021 vehicle wreck. He did not play competitively again that year.

2024 back surgery

Woods has microdecompression surgery of the lumbar region of his back in September.

2025 Achilles tear and back surgery

Woods ruptured the tendon in March 2025, preventing him from playing in any majors that year. In October, Woods had his seventh back surgery, a lumbar disc replacement.

Sources: USA TODAY Sports, Golf Digest, ESPN

search_result.title: Tiger Woods arrest details: Struggled following directions after crash
search_result.text:
Updated March 31, 2026, 1:47 p.m. ET

Tiger Woods was involved in a two-vehicle rollover crash and charged with DUI with property damage.

Deputies observed signs of impairment, including bloodshot eyes, lethargy, and difficulty with coordination exercises.

Woods passed a breathalyzer test but refused a urinalysis, and police found two hydrocodone pills in his pocket.

(This story was updated to add new information.)

Tiger Woods had difficulty following directions while taking sobriety tests and was carrying two pills revealed to be hydrocodone following his rollover crash in Jupiter Island on March 27, according to the Martin County Sheriff's Office.

Woods, the 15-time golf major champion and a Jupiter Island resident, said he had looked down at his cellphone and was changing radio stations as he drove his black Land Rover along South Beach Road when he struck a trailer attached to the Ford F-150 he was passing.

The impact caused Woods to lose control of the Land Rover, which flipped onto its driver's side, according to a report made public on March 31.

Woods, 50, is facing charges of DUI with property damage and refusal to submit to a lawful test before being released from jail that night and driven home in a private vehicle.

The wreck was the second time since 2021 that Woods has been in a crash in which his vehicle rolled over and the second time since 2017 he has been arrested on DUI charges.

Court records indicate Woods' arraignment — which the golfer will not be required to attend — will be April 23 before County Judge Kathleen Roberts. It will follow the final round of the Masters, the first major tournament on the PGA Tour calendar this year, by 11 days.

Woods, who had back surgery in October, has stated his desire to play the Masters, but that was before the crash. Although he remains listed in the field — a formality as a former winner of the tournament — it is unknown if he will be at August National Golf Club for the tournament that starts April 9.

Woods' five Masters titles is second all-time to North Palm Beach resident Jack Nicklaus' six.

What Tiger Woods, other driver said after crash

The sheriff's report states that Woods was trailing the Ford F-150 on the 200 block of South Beach Road, near the Hobe Sound Yacht Club, when the pickup slowed down to make a right turn into a driveway.

Woods then headed into oncoming traffic, traveling over the double solid line and struck the attached trailer with his SUV's front right bumper, sending the vehicle tumbling onto its side. The report does not indicate how fast either vehicle was traveling when the wreck happened.

Woods told a deputy he did not realize the vehicle in front of him had slowed down when he looked away from the road. He said he was coming from his Jupiter Island home.

The driver of the Ford said he activated his turn signal. He and another person helped Woods out of the passenger's side. The trailer was estimated to have received about $5,000 worth of damage.

Neither party sustained injuries and both refused to be taken to a hospital, according to the sheriff's report.

While speaking to Woods, the deputy observed signs of impairment and requested a DUI investigation to test Woods was driving in a safe manner at the time of the collision.

Woods then learned the case had gone from a crash investigation to a criminal DUI investigation.

Tiger Woods 'sweating profusely' speaking to deputies after crash

Woods was observed to be "sweating profusely" and "lethargic and slow" while speaking with deputies. The report also states he was "extremely alert." At that time, deputies were unable to look at Woods' eyes because he had put on his sunglasses.

The deputy then observed Woods limping and stumbling to the right as he was walking to the deputy's car. He then was asked about any injuries and said he had seven back surgeries and more than 20 operations on his leg.

Woods was asked if he was able to perform tasks such as walking and lifting his leg and said he has a limp and that one of his ankle seizes while walking.

Woods was asked to sit on the push bumper of the patrol car and asked to remove his sunglasses. The deputy then observed Woods' eyes to be "bloodshot and glassy" and his pupils "extremely dilated."

When asked if he had consumed alcohol, Woods said "none." When asked if he consumed any prescription medication, Woods said, "I take a few," and said he had taken his prescription medication in the morning.

Woods then was given several exercises, including follow the tip of a pen, placing his finger on his nose, palm pat and hand coordination.

On each test, he had to be reminded at least once to follow directions.

The deputy wrote that based on his observations he believed Woods' normal faculties were impaired and he was unable to safely operate the motor vehicle.

Deputies then placed Woods under arrest.

A search of Woods' pocket found two pills revealed to be hydrocodone, a strong opioid painkiller to treat moderate to severe pain. Deputies took the pills and submitted them into evidence.

Once inside the DUI room at the Martin County Jail, Woods submitted to a breath test, which he passed with a 0.000 score, but he refused a urinalysis, leading to a charge of refusal to submit, according to the sheriff's report.

Deputies drove Woods to Cleveland Clinic in Stuart while he was in custody because he had been involved in a traffic crash. He refused all medial treatment and returned to the jail.

search_result.title: New details in Tiger Woods arrest in Florida; affidavit released
search_result.text:
Updated March 31, 2026, 1:36 p.m. ET

Tiger Woods told investigators he was looking at his cell phone before a rollover crash.

Woods was arrested on a DUI charge and for refusing to submit to a DUI test.

Police found two hydrocodone pills in Woods' pocket following the incident.

Professional golf legend Tiger Woods told Martin County Sheriff's investigators he was looking down at his cell phone and changing the radio station before he is accused of crashing into another vehicle and rolling his sport utility vehicle on its side.

Woods, 50, ultimately would be arrested on DUI and refusal to submit to a DUI test following the March 27 crash on Jupiter Island in extreme southeast Martin County. He was described as "sweating profusely" when speaking with investigators after the crash.

These and other details were contained in the arrest affidavit for Eldrick T. Woods released March 31 by the Martin County Sheriff's Office.

Tiger Woods arrest: What deputies say they discovered

Deputies about 2 p.m. went to an address in the 200 block of South Beach Road on Jupiter Island following the crash.

Investigators reported a Ford F-150 pickup with an attached trailer travelled north on South Beach Road as a black Land Rover was behind it.

The pickup slowed to turn right into a driveway, and the Land Rover, which investigators said was driven by Woods, traveled over the double solid line into oncoming traffic, the affidavit states.

The Land Rover struck the rear left fender to the trailer that was attached to the pickup.

The Land Rover “lost control and flipped on the driver’s side,” the affidavit states.

Woods told investigators he looked down at his cell phone, and didn’t realize the pickup had slowed. He reported no injuries.

The pickup driver told investigators he activated his right turn signal before making the turn.

He said the sport utility vehicle behind him “must not have seen him slowing down and struck his vehicle,” the affidavit states.

The pickup driver said he and another person helped the SUV driver, identified as Woods, out.

The pickup driver said Woods had to exit the SUV via the front passenger side as it was flipped on the driver’s side.

Investigators noted Woods was “sweating profusely.”

Efforts by TCPalm to reach the pickup driver March 31 in person and also via phone were not successful.

What deputies say Tiger Woods looked like after crash

“I inquired about the collision, to which Woods stated he was looking down at his cell phone and changing the radio station and did not notice the vehicle in front of him had slowed down,” the affidavit states. “Woods stated he was coming from his house.”

Woods has lived in Jupiter Island since 2007, when he purchased his property for $44.5 million, according to Martin County property appraiser records.

Investigators reported Woods’ movement was “lethargic and slow,” and he was “extremely alert.”

Woods agreed to participate in field sobriety exercises.

“While walking, I observed Woods limping and stumbling to the right,” the affidavit states.

He looked to have a leg injury and a compression sock on his right knee.

Asked if he had any medical conditions, Woods reported he’s had seven back surgeries and more than 20 operations involving his leg.

He said he has a limp and his ankle seizes when he walks.

Investigators instructed Woods to take off his sunglasses. His eyes were glassy and bloodshot, and his pupils “extremely dialated,” records show.

Woods reported he had not consumed any alcoholic beverages.

Asked if he took any prescription medication, Woods said, “I take a few.”

He said “he takes medication for high blood pressure, cholesterol, ibuprofen and Vicodin.”

Ibuprofen can treat mild to moderate pain, arthritis or inflammation, according to Cleveland Clinic.

A prescription painkiller, Vicodin contains hydrocodone and acetaminophen, according to the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration.

Woods told investigators he took his prescription medication earlier in the morning.

“While speaking to Woods, I observed him to be extremely alert and talkative,” records show. “Woods had hiccups during the entire investigation.”

Woods said he had not consumed any “illegal substances.”

Following additional exercises, investigators determined Woods’ “normal faculties were impaired, and he was unable to safely operate the motor vehicle,” an affidavit shows.

What happened after Tiger Woods was arrested

About 3:12 p.m., Woods was told he was under arrest. Investigators reported finding two hydrocodone pills in his pants pocket. Hydrocodone is a prescription painkiller.

At the Martin County Jail, Woods took a breath test to measure blood-alcohol content. That test twice measured 0.00.

A reading of 0.08 and greater is considered illegal.

Woods declined to submit to a urinalysis test to determine “chemical or controlled substances” in his system.

Medical staff at the jail asked that Woods be taken to Cleveland Clinic Martin South Hospital for “medical clearance” because he was in a crash.

Woods was taken there and declined treatment. He was returned to the jail.

Woods was released at 11:11 p.m. March 27 from the Martin County jail on $1,150 bail, a jail official said.

(This story has been updated with additional information, photos and video.)

Will Greenlee is a breaking news reporter for TCPalm. Follow Will on X @OffTheBeatTweet or reach him by phone at 772-267-7926. E-mail him at will.greenlee@tcpalm.com.

Justin Bieber is returning to the Grammys stage.

For the first time in four years, the "Yukon" singer will perform at the annual award show on Sunday, Feb. 1, the Grammy Awards announced. Bieber, whose album "Swag" is nominated for album of the year and best pop vocal album, plus a best pop solo performance nod for "Daises" and best R&B performance nod for "Yukon," last performed and attended the ceremony in 2022.

Previously announced performers include Olivia Dean, Addison Rae, KATSEYE and Leon Thomas, who will join the full list of best new artists nominees in a new segment at the awards show, as well as nominees Sabrina Carpenter and Clipse, with Pharrell.

Grammys all-time records that could be made or broken in 2026

July's "Swag," a pop and R&B record featuring 21 tracks with three interludes, peaked at No. 2 on the Billboard 200 album charts, while "Daisies" peaked at No. 2 and spent 27 weeks on the Billboard Hot 100. Bieber followed the album with "Swag II" in September.

Bieber – who last performed his Grammy-nominated track "Peaches" at the award show – has taken a lengthy hiatus from performing, appearing on stage sparingly since revealing in 2022 that he was diagnosed with Ramsay Hunt syndrome. Months after the diagnosis, the 31-year-old singer postponed and eventually canceled the remainder of his Justice World Tour dates. That tour also came after a lengthy hiatus, following his 2016-17 Purpose World Tour.

"I performed six live shows, but it took a real toll on me," Bieber said at the time. "So I'm going to take a break from touring for the time being. I'm going to be OK."

search_result.title: Blue Eyed Bettys return to their musical roots at FST
search_result.text:
Vicki Dean

vdeanfla@gmail.com

July 15, 2017 Updated July 16, 2017, 10:53 a.m. ET

The Blue Eyed Bettys are returning to the stage where their band was born with a month-long run of shows this summer at Florida Studio Theatre’s Court Cabaret.

The group is made up of three singing, instrument-playing actors who were living in New York when they were individually cast in a 2014 FST cabaret revue called “Poems, Prayers and Promises” and discovered they had natural three-part harmonies. The show featured songs from the 1960s through the 1980s written by American folk rock singer-songwriters like Paul Simon, Carole King, John Denver and Harry Chapin.

“We just kind of all hit it off right away, became fast friends, started hanging out after the shows and then we started playing music together,” said Sarah Hund, who sings and plays fiddle in the Blue Eyed Bettys. “Eventually, we started writing music together and suddenly we were a band.”

Naming a new band is never easy and the group sort of stumbled upon a moniker after noting all three band members have blue eyes. Hund, banjo player Daniel Emond and guitar player Ben Mackel, started playing open mikes at such Sarasota venues as Cock & Bull, Classico and Starlite.

“One day at an open mike it just came out. ‘We’re the Blue Eyed Bettys’ and it stuck,” Hund said. “Somehow it’s worked for us.”

Bettys’ sound

The trio has a throwback folk feel, but features a more animated on-stage delivery from the trained actors in the band. With the acoustic instrument mix, the sound is difficult to categorize.

“We say we’re a harmony string band because we sing a lot of three-part harmony,” she said. “It’s sort of in the Indie folk pop genre, I guess, but the basic blanket is Americana.”

During the 2014 show, they found that three-part harmonies came naturally and the more they worked together, the better they sounded. When the musicians started writing their own material, at times they had to work harder on the harmonies. Other times, the blending of their three voices just seemed to happen naturally.

“A lot of times it just sort of comes out and we like what we hear. We call that Betty magic,” Hund said.

New album

While all three have impressive acting credentials at theaters along the east coast from Florida to New York, they are working on writing their own material for a new Blue Eyed Bettys recording project due to be released in the fall.

“It’s a nice outlet for all of us to be able to perform our own work,” Hund said.

Hund said she has been playing violin and fiddle since she was 10, and has taken music lessons on and off through adulthood. She stuck with it after college when she found she could get more acting jobs if she played an instrument.

Emond, a classically trained pianist, picked up banjo when college friends formed a bluegrass band. Mackel also started playing guitar in college.

A review by Herald-Tribune Arts Editor Jay Handelman of the show, which also included two other performers, noted how well the group played together. “They blend nicely in the catchy and smooth arrangements by musical director Ben Krauss, and sound good on solos and duets. More impressively, most of them play multiple instruments, easily switching from piano or guitar to mandolin, bongos, banjo and others,” he wrote.

Back to SRQ

All three are excited to return to Sarasota and FST this week, Hund said. The show will feature familiar covers from the Beatles to Fleetwood Mac along with some original material.

“We’ve tried to put our own spin on the FST Cabaret model,” Hund said. “I think it will be an eclectic mix of tunes and I think there will be something for everyone in there, a lot of harmonies, a lot of banjo, fiddle and guitar solos.”

The Bettys will be joined by Kroy Presley on upright bass to round out the sound. Presley was featured in FST’s production of “Million Dollar Quartet.”

Band members love working here because of how the Sarasota community supports the arts, from opera to ballet to music, she said.

“We couldn’t have found a better city to start a band in because of all the support we received when we first started out,” Hund said. “We’re super excited to come back.”

Billy Joel has normal pressure hydrocephalus. What is that, and what are symptoms?

Billy Joel diagnosed with NPH

Normal pressure hydrocephalus, or NPH, is a disorder in which excess fluid accumulates in the brain, causing issues with thinking and reasoning as well as sometimes walking and bladder control, according to the Alzheimer's Association.

'My health must come first': Billy Joel surgery recovery delays 8 concerts

"Billy is thankful for the excellent care he is receiving and is fully committed to prioritizing his health," the social media statement continued. "He is grateful for the support from fans during this time and looks forward to the day when he can once again take the stage."

What happens to Billy Joel concerts? What we know about refunds

A beloved beacon of a bygone rock era, Joel can play both punkster and balladeer. With tracks like "Just the Way You Are" and "Piano Man," he channels a sentimental and poetic tone, a harsh departure from other tracks like "My Life" and "You May Be Right," which offer cutting lyrics and a working-class, east-coast sensibility.
